---
title: "Day 6 — Exercise: Fetch sequences from the backend"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Wire the frontend to the axum backend that has been running in the background all day. On mount, fetch the list of samples from `/api/samples` and show one button per record. Clicking a button fetches the full record from `/api/samples/{id}` and drops its sequence into the textarea, so all the statistics from [exercise 2](02-reactive-stats.qmd) light up automatically.

This is the first time you will use **`async`/`await`** in this course. We will teach it as a single, narrowly-scoped pattern: "this is how you do a fetch in WASM". The deep dive into `Future`, executors, and Tokio is explicitly *not* in scope today.

## Background

A `.wasm` blob has no threads. It cannot block on a synchronous network call — there is nowhere for it to "wait". Instead, the browser runs your WASM code in cooperative chunks: every call to `await` is a *yield point* where your function pauses, the browser runs other tasks, and your function later resumes when the awaited thing is ready.

[`wasm_bindgen_futures::spawn_local`](https://docs.rs/wasm-bindgen-futures/latest/wasm_bindgen_futures/fn.spawn_local.html) is the bridge: it takes an `async` block and hands it to the browser's event loop. Inside the block, anything ending with `.await` is fair game.

[`gloo_net::http::Request`](https://docs.rs/gloo-net/0.7.0/gloo_net/http/struct.Request.html) is the WASM-side fetch wrapper. The pattern is the same as JavaScript's `fetch().then(r => r.json())`, but in Rust:

```rust
let resp = Request::get(url).send().await?;
let data: T = resp.json().await?;
```

Two `await`s: one to wait for the HTTP response headers and status; one to wait for the body to be fully received and deserialised by `serde_json`. The `T` you deserialise into has to live in the `shared` crate (or be re-defined identically here) — but we already have `SampleSummary` and `SampleRecord` there, ready to use.

![A single round-trip when the user picks a sample. The Yew app, running as WebAssembly inside the page, issues `GET /api/samples/42` from inside an `async` block scheduled by `spawn_local`. axum looks the record up, returns a JSON body `{id, name, sequence}`, and the Yew component writes the sequence into the same `typed` state that the textarea is bound to — so the stats panel from exercise 2 re-renders for free. Open the browser devtools' Network panel and you should see exactly one row per click, matching the solid request arrow above.](images/fetch-sequence-diagram.svg){#fig-fetch-sequence fig-alt="UML sequence diagram with three lifelines (User in grey, Browser/Yew app in blue, axum backend in green). A click on a 'Load sample 42' button triggers a GET /api/samples/42, axum reads a row from DB or CSV and returns 200 OK with a JSON body, the Yew app updates state and re-renders, and the sequence becomes visible to the user."}

## The exercise

```bash
cd day6/ex-sequence-inspector
```

You are still editing `frontend/src/main.rs`. Trunk should still be running; the backend should still be running in the other terminal (from [exercise 1](01-hello-yew.qmd)).

By the end of this exercise, the page should show a list of sample names as buttons; clicking one populates the textarea with the matching DNA and updates the statistics live, plus shows a "Loaded: {name}" panel below the table.

## Step by step

### Step 1 — bring the shared types into scope

At the top of `frontend/src/main.rs`, add the imports you will need:

```rust
use gloo_net::http::Request;
use shared::{SampleRecord, SampleSummary};
```

The `shared::*` import is the wire-format payoff: the exact types the backend serialises into JSON are the exact types the frontend deserialises. If you change a field name in `shared/src/lib.rs`, both crates fail to compile until they agree again — drift between client and server becomes a compiler error.

### Step 2 — add state for the sample list and the selected record

Inside `app()`, before `typed`, add two new pieces of state:

```rust
let samples = use_state(Vec::<SampleSummary>::new);
let selected = use_state(|| None::<SampleRecord>);
```

[`Vec::<SampleSummary>::new`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.new) is passed as a function (no parentheses) — `use_state` calls it once to produce the initial value. Same for the closure `|| None::<SampleRecord>` which builds the initial `None`.

### Step 3 — load the sample list on mount

Right after the state declarations, add an effect that runs once when the component first appears on screen:

```rust
{
    let samples = samples.clone();
    use_effect_with((), move |_| {
        wasm_bindgen_futures::spawn_local(async move {
            if let Ok(resp) = Request::get("/api/samples").send().await {
                if let Ok(data) = resp.json::<Vec<SampleSummary>>().await {
                    samples.set(data);
                }
            }
        });
        || ()
    });
}
```

A lot is happening here. Read it bottom-up:

- The outer block `{ ... }` exists only to keep this temporary `let samples = samples.clone();` out of the surrounding scope. Yew components are full of these tiny scopes.
- [`use_effect_with(deps, body)`](https://docs.rs/yew/0.23.0/yew/functional/fn.use_effect_with.html) runs `body` whenever `deps` change. We pass `()` so the deps never change — i.e. the body runs exactly once on first render.
- [`spawn_local`](https://docs.rs/wasm-bindgen-futures/latest/wasm_bindgen_futures/fn.spawn_local.html) schedules the inner `async move { ... }` on the browser's event loop. The future runs asynchronously while React-style rendering continues.
- Inside the async block, the two `if let Ok(...)` patterns are a compact way to swallow errors (network failure, bad JSON, …). For a real app you would surface them in the UI; for the course exercise it is enough to display nothing in the failure case.
- The `|| ()` at the end is the *cleanup* closure that `use_effect_with` requires. We have nothing to clean up, so it just returns the unit type.

### Step 4 — display the sample list as buttons

We will declare the `on_pick` callback in a minute; for now, just render the list. Add this to the top of the `html!` block, above the textarea:

```rust
<h2>{ "Pick a sample" }</h2>
<ul>
    { for samples.iter().map(|s| html! {
        <li><button>{ &s.name }</button></li>
    }) }
</ul>
```

`{ for ... }` is Yew's syntax for rendering an iterator of `Html` nodes. Save the file. After trunk reloads, you should see a list of five buttons under "Pick a sample". They do nothing yet — clicking is wired up next.

### Step 5 — add the `on_pick` callback

Below the `on_input` callback you already have, add `on_pick`. It takes the sample's `id`, fetches the full record, and pushes the sequence into the textarea state:

```rust
let on_pick = {
    let selected = selected.clone();
    let typed = typed.clone();
    Callback::from(move |id: String| {
        let selected = selected.clone();
        let typed = typed.clone();
        wasm_bindgen_futures::spawn_local(async move {
            let url = format!("/api/samples/{}", id);
            if let Ok(resp) = Request::get(&url).send().await {
                if let Ok(data) = resp.json::<SampleRecord>().await {
                    typed.set(data.sequence.clone());
                    selected.set(Some(data));
                }
            }
        });
    })
};
```

The double-clone (`let selected = selected.clone();` outside the closure, then again inside) is annoying but necessary: the outer `Callback::from` itself moves `selected` once, and then *each invocation* of the callback needs its own clone to move into the inner async block.

### Step 6 — wire the buttons to `on_pick`

Replace the placeholder `<button>` element from step 4 with a click handler that emits the sample id:

```rust
<h2>{ "Pick a sample" }</h2>
<ul>
    { for samples.iter().map(|s| {
        let id = s.id.clone();
        let on_pick = on_pick.clone();
        html! {
            <li>
                <button onclick={move |_| on_pick.emit(id.clone())}>
                    { &s.name }
                </button>
            </li>
        }
    })}
</ul>
```

Each list item gets its own copy of the id and the callback. Click a button: the callback runs, the fetch goes out, and on the next render the textarea is populated and the statistics update. **None of the keystroke logic from exercise 2 needs to change** — the fetch result writes to the same `typed` state.

### Step 7 — show the loaded record

Below the statistics table in the `html!` block, add a panel that displays the loaded record (if any):

```rust
{
    if let Some(rec) = &*selected {
        html! {
            <>
                <h2>{ format!("Loaded: {}", rec.name) }</h2>
                <div class="sequence">{ &rec.sequence }</div>
            </>
        }
    } else {
        html! {}
    }
}
```

`&*selected` dereferences the `UseStateHandle<Option<SampleRecord>>` and then borrows the inner value. The `<></>` fragment wraps two siblings (`<h2>` and `<div>`) without adding an extra DOM element.

Save. Click a sample. The textarea fills, the stats update, the "Loaded: …" panel appears.

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — why both `if let Ok(...)` rather than `?`"}
We are inside an `async` block passed to `spawn_local`. That block has return type `()` — there is no place for the `?` operator to bail out to. For a teaching example we just nest the success cases. In a real app you would extract the fetch into a function returning `Result<T, E>` so `?` works:

```rust
async fn load_samples() -> Result<Vec<SampleSummary>, gloo_net::Error> {
    let resp = Request::get("/api/samples").send().await?;
    let data = resp.json::<Vec<SampleSummary>>().await?;
    Ok(data)
}
```

…then the `spawn_local` callsite calls `load_samples().await` and inspects the `Result` once.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — nothing happens, no error in the terminal"}
First place to look: the browser DevTools network panel (F12, "Network" tab). You should see two requests:

- `/api/samples` — fires on page load, returns the JSON list with HTTP 200.
- `/api/samples/{id}` — fires when you click a button.

If neither fires: trunk is not running, or your `on_pick` callback was never wired to the button's `onclick`. If only the first fires but the second does not, the click handler is wrong.

If a request fires but the response is HTTP 404 or 500: check the backend terminal — axum prints requests as they come in. If the request never reached the backend, your Trunk proxy config is wrong; re-read `frontend/Trunk.toml`.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — \"borrow of moved value\" or \"cannot move out\" errors"}
Yew's reactive model copies and clones a lot of small state handles, and the borrow checker takes a while to get used to. Two rules of thumb:

- Clone state handles freely. `UseStateHandle<T>` is internally an `Rc`, so `.clone()` bumps a counter — it is cheap and idiomatic.
- The pattern `let x = x.clone();` immediately before a `move` closure that needs its own `x` is so common it has its own informal name (the "rebinding shuffle"). Embrace it.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="The full `frontend/src/main.rs` at the end of this exercise"}
The fully wired-up version (after exercise 4) is in `day6/ex-sequence-inspector-solution/frontend/src/main.rs`. The state at the end of *this* exercise is the same minus the SVG plot block — everything else is in place.
:::

## Where this leads

- [Exercise 4](04-sequence-inspector.qmd), the capstone: add an inline SVG plot of GC content over a sliding window. The same `typed` state is the input; the plot updates live for both pasted sequences and fetched samples.
- Sharing types via the `shared` crate is the same pattern you will see in real Rust full-stack stacks like [Leptos](https://leptos.dev) + axum or [Dioxus](https://dioxuslabs.com) + axum. The exact crates change; the wire-format argument does not.
